name: Ballistic Missile Arsenal — Shahab Series (Shahab-1/2/3)
description: The Shahab series constitutes the oldest tier of Iran's ballistic missile force. Shahab-1 (300 km range, Scud-B derivative) and Shahab-2 (500 km, Scud-C derivative) are liquid-fueled short-range systems primarily useful against Gulf state targets. Shahab-3 (1,300–1,950 km) can reach Israel and is the backbone of Iran's medium-range deterrent, though its liquid-fuel propulsion requires extended launch preparation time (hours), making it vulnerable to preemptive strikes on transporter-erector-launchers (TELs). Israel struck TEL parks and missile production facilities in both October 2024 and the June 2025 Twelve-Day War, destroying an estimated 120 TELs during the latter conflict. By January 2026, Iran has reconstituted approximately 2,500 missiles total across all types (IDF assessment), up from ~1,500 post-June 2025, reflecting production capacity of 50–100 missiles per month sustained by Chinese-supplied sodium perchlorate (solid fuel precursor). In a conflict with the US and Israel, Shahab-series missiles would be used in large salvos to saturate air defenses, though their accuracy (CEP ~2 km for older variants) limits them to area targets such as airbases, ports, and cities.
unit: missiles (all types combined, January 2026 IDF estimate)
political_cost: Launch authority rests with the Supreme Leader through the IRGC Aerospace Force; the regular military (Artesh) has no independent launch authority, creating command friction if decapitation occurs.
source: Gen. McKenzie (CENTCOM, pre-June 2025 baseline of ~3,000); IDF post-war estimates; IISS Military Balance; CTP-ISW Iran updates

name: Ballistic Missile — Emad (MRBM, maneuverable reentry vehicle)
description: The Emad is a Shahab-3 derivative with a maneuverable reentry vehicle (MaRV), giving it improved terminal accuracy (CEP estimated at several hundred meters versus ~2 km for baseline Shahab-3). Range is approximately 1,700 km, sufficient to reach Israel from western Iran. The MaRV complicates interception by terminal-phase missile defense systems such as Arrow-2/3, as the warhead can execute evasive maneuvers during descent. The Emad was first tested in 2015 and has been produced in quantity. In a conflict scenario, Emad would be used against high-value fixed targets such as Israeli airbases (Nevatim, Ramon), critical infrastructure, and C2 nodes. Its liquid-fuel propulsion remains a vulnerability—launch preparation takes hours, and Iran's TEL fleet was significantly degraded during the June 2025 war. Iran has been reconstituting TEL stocks during the interwar period, but exact numbers are uncertain. The IRGC Aerospace Force controls these assets.
unit: exact inventory classified; subset of ~2,500 total arsenal
political_cost: Use against Israeli civilian centers would be a major escalation threshold; IRGC hardliners favor maximum retaliation while some political figures prefer calibrated responses to preserve diplomatic options.
source: IISS Military Balance; CSIS Missile Defense Project; CRS reports on Iranian ballistic missiles

name: Ballistic Missile — Khorramshahr / Kheibar Shekan (solid-fuel MRBM)
description: The Kheibar Shekan represents Iran's shift toward solid-fuel medium-range ballistic missiles, which are faster to launch (minutes vs. hours for liquid-fuel), more mobile, and harder to detect in pre-launch phase. Range is approximately 1,450 km with a maneuverable warhead. The Khorramshahr (liquid-fuel, ~2,000 km, potentially 1,500 kg payload) is a separate heavier system with greater payload capacity. Israel specifically targeted solid-fuel missile production infrastructure in October 2024, destroying an estimated 12–20 planetary mixers used to produce solid propellant at facilities including Shahroud. IISS analyst Fabian Hinz assessed this as a 'significant blow' to production capacity, with US estimates suggesting at least a year to rebuild. However, Iran received approximately 3,000 tons of sodium perchlorate from China during the interwar period and has been aggressively reconstituting production. By January 2026, solid-fuel missile production has partially resumed but likely not at pre-October 2024 rates. These missiles would be priority weapons in any conflict due to their survivability.
unit: exact inventory unknown; assessed as hundreds within total arsenal
political_cost: Solid-fuel missile production is a strategic priority for the IRGC; any diplomatic concession affecting this capability faces intense internal resistance from the IRGC Aerospace Force.
source: IISS (Fabian Hinz analysis); Carnegie Endowment; FPRI 'Shallow Ramparts' analysis (November 2025); CTP-ISW

name: Ballistic Missile — Sejjil (solid-fuel IRBM)
description: The Sejjil is Iran's longest-range solid-fuel ballistic missile, with a reported range of 2,000–2,500 km and a two-stage design. It represents Iran's most advanced indigenous solid-fuel technology and can reach any target in Israel, as well as US bases across the entire Middle East and parts of southeastern Europe. Its solid-fuel propulsion allows rapid launch with minimal preparation time, making it highly survivable against preemptive strikes. Production numbers are uncertain but believed to be more limited than the Shahab series due to manufacturing complexity. The Sejjil was used during the June 2025 war as part of Iran's retaliatory salvos. In a future conflict, Sejjil would likely be reserved for high-value strategic targets due to its relatively limited inventory. Its range also gives Iran escalation options against targets in Turkey or southeastern NATO territory, though such use would be an extreme escalation.
unit: limited inventory; exact numbers unknown
political_cost: Use against non-Israeli, non-US targets (e.g., Gulf states, Turkey) would dramatically expand the conflict and is constrained by strategic calculation rather than technical limitation.
source: IISS Military Balance; CSIS Missile Defense Project; NASIC Ballistic and Cruise Missile Threat assessment

name: Hypersonic / Advanced Missile — Fattah Series (Fattah-1, Fattah-2)
description: The Fattah series represents Iran's most advanced missile technology. Fattah-1, unveiled in June 2023, is claimed to have a range of 1,400 km with a maneuverable hypersonic reentry vehicle at speeds of Mach 13–15 (Iranian claims; Western analysts are skeptical of these speed figures). Fattah-2, revealed later, is equipped with what Iran describes as a true hypersonic glide vehicle (HGV), representing a qualitative leap if functional as advertised. These weapons pose an acute interception challenge: the SM-6 interceptor travels at approximately Mach 4, creating a significant speed mismatch. Inventory is believed to be very limited as of January 2026, likely in the low dozens at most. Iran would reserve these for the highest-value targets where penetrating advanced air defenses is essential—such as Israeli command centers, THAAD radar installations, or carrier strike groups. The Fattah program is a prestige project for the IRGC Aerospace Force and has received maximum resource priority.
unit: limited inventory, likely low dozens; exact count unknown
political_cost: The Fattah program is a source of IRGC institutional pride and a key deterrent; its use early in a conflict would sacrifice strategic surprise, creating tension between immediate retaliation impulses and conservation for escalation dominance.
source: Iranian state media (IRGC Aerospace Force); CSIS analysis; Western skepticism documented in IISS and FPRI assessments

name: Cruise Missile Arsenal (Soumar, Hoveyzeh, Abu Mahdi)
description: Iran possesses a growing cruise missile force that complements its ballistic missile arsenal. The Soumar (ground-launched, 700–2,500 km range depending on variant, derived from Soviet Kh-55 technology obtained from Ukraine) and Hoveyzeh (~1,350 km range) provide land-attack capability at lower altitudes than ballistic missiles, complicating air defense geometry. The Abu Mahdi is a naval-launched anti-ship cruise missile variant with an estimated range of ~1,000 km. These systems fly at subsonic to low-supersonic speeds at low altitude, making them difficult to detect with ground-based radars in terrain-masked environments. Exact inventory is unknown but described by CENTCOM as a 'burgeoning force' not included in the ~3,000 ballistic missile count. In a conflict, cruise missiles would be used alongside ballistic missiles and drones in complex multi-domain salvos designed to saturate defenses from multiple vectors and altitudes simultaneously. Their lower radar cross-section and terrain-following capability make them particularly effective against Gulf state targets across short overwater distances.
unit: inventory not publicly quantified; described as 'burgeoning' by CENTCOM
political_cost: Cruise missile strikes on Gulf state energy infrastructure would inflict massive economic damage on Iran's own potential customers and diplomatic partners, constraining their use to military targets unless the conflict becomes existential.
source: CENTCOM assessments; IISS Military Balance; CSIS Missile Defense Project

name: Shahed Drone Arsenal (Shahed-136, Shahed-131, Shahed-107)
description: Iran's one-way attack (OWA) drone fleet is its most strategically consequential asymmetric weapon system. The Shahed-136 (2,000 km range, 30–50 kg warhead, GPS-guided, ~$20,000–$50,000 per unit, most commonly cited at ~$35,000) is the primary variant. The Shahed-131 is a smaller variant and the Shahed-107 functions as a loitering munition. Israeli intelligence assessed pre-conflict stockpiles at approximately 80,000 combat-ready airframes (treat as upper estimate with significant uncertainty). Peacetime production rate is 200–500 units/month, with surge capacity assessed at ~400/day with Russian technical assistance. Manufacturing is dispersed across multiple underground facilities using commercial components, making the production base highly resilient to strikes. Russia has provided technical modifications improving targeting, handling, and flight profiles based on combat experience in Ukraine. The cost asymmetry is the defining strategic feature: defenders must expend $3.7–15 million interceptors against $35,000 drones, creating a 100:1 to 350:1 cost ratio that is operationally unsustainable over weeks of conflict.
unit: combat-ready airframes (Israeli intelligence upper estimate; significant uncertainty)
political_cost: Drone transfers to Russia for use in Ukraine have already drawn international condemnation; large-scale use against civilian targets in a US/Israel conflict would strengthen the case for Western escalation but the cost asymmetry favors Iran regardless.
source: Israeli intelligence assessments; CSIS; IISS; CENTCOM cost estimates; Bloomberg reporting on dispersed manufacturing

name: Air Defense — Post-October 2024 Recovery Status
description: Iran's air defense posture was severely degraded by the October 2024 Israeli strikes, which destroyed all four of Iran's Russian-made S-300PMU2 batteries—its most capable systems. A Biden administration adviser described Iran as 'essentially naked' after the strikes. Iran also lost at least two early-warning radar systems in Ilam and Khuzestan provinces. Through early 2025, Iran undertook intensive recovery efforts: in February 2025, the IRIADF demonstrated integration of surviving S-300 components (radars, launchers) with the indigenous Bavar-373 system. The Bavar-373-II was unveiled with extended 300 km engagement range, autonomous TELAR units with integrated radar, and Sayyad-4B missiles. Satellite imagery from early 2026 showed S-300 launcher units reappearing near Tehran, though whether these represent restored operational capability or deceptive positioning is debated. Iran also received S-400 components from Russia, though the delivery timeline and operational status are uncertain. By January 2026, Iran's air defense is assessed as partially reconstituted but still significantly degraded compared to pre-October 2024 levels, relying primarily on domestically produced Bavar-373 variants rather than the destroyed Russian systems.
unit: mix of Bavar-373 batteries, surviving/reconstituted S-300 components, point-defense systems (Khordad-15, Majid, Arman)
political_cost: Air defense vulnerability is an existential concern for regime survival; dependence on Russian resupply creates leverage for Moscow while the push for indigenous alternatives (Bavar-373) reflects IRGC desire for self-sufficiency.
source: Times of Israel; Fox News (US/Israeli officials); FPRI 'Shallow Ramparts'; Janes (Feb 2025 S-300/Bavar integration); Defence Security Asia (satellite imagery Feb 2026); CTP-ISW (July 2025); Al Jazeera (March 2025 drills)

name: Naval — Strait of Hormuz Denial Capabilities (Mine Warfare)
description: Iran possesses an estimated 5,000–6,000 naval mines of various types including contact, influence, and rising mines. This represents one of the largest mine arsenals in the world and is the centerpiece of Iran's Strait of Hormuz denial strategy. The Strait is 21 nautical miles wide at its narrowest, with navigable shipping channels even narrower, making it highly vulnerable to mining. Iran's mine inventory includes unsophisticated contact mines, more advanced influence mines triggered by magnetic or acoustic signatures, and rising mines that sit on the seabed and launch upward when detecting a target. Mine warfare is Iran's most cost-effective area-denial capability: mines cost thousands of dollars each but can disable or sink vessels worth billions, and mine clearance operations take months even after the military threat is removed. Iran's IRGC Navy has trained extensively for covert mine-laying operations using small boats, commercial vessels, and even submarines. In a conflict, rapid mining of the Strait would be among Iran's first actions, as it creates a persistent threat that outlasts any air campaign.
unit: naval mines (estimated upper range; contact, influence, and rising types)
political_cost: Mining the Strait would disrupt Iran's own oil exports and those of partners like China; however, Iran has demonstrated willingness to accept this cost when facing existential military threats, viewing Hormuz closure as its strongest economic weapon.
source: CENTCOM assessments; IISS Military Balance; ONI (Office of Naval Intelligence) reports on Iranian naval capabilities; CSIS

name: Naval — IRGC Navy Fast Attack Craft and Anti-Ship Missiles
description: The IRGC Navy operates hundreds of fast attack craft and fast inshore attack craft (FIAC), forming the backbone of Iran's asymmetric naval doctrine in the Persian Gulf and Strait of Hormuz. These include armed speedboats carrying anti-ship missiles, rockets, and machine guns, designed for swarm tactics against larger warships. Iran's anti-ship missile inventory includes the Noor (120 km range, C-802 derivative), Qader (200–300 km), Khalij Fars (300 km anti-ship ballistic missile with electro-optical seeker), and Hormuz-2 (radar-homing variant). Iran has also developed unmanned surface vessels (USVs) for kamikaze-style attacks on shipping. The IRGC Navy is doctrinally separate from the regular navy (Artesh Navy, which operates conventional frigates and submarines in blue water) and controls Persian Gulf and Strait operations. In a conflict, IRGC fast boats would conduct swarm attacks in the confined waters of the Strait, using islands, coastal terrain, and commercial shipping as concealment. Anti-ship coastal missile batteries positioned along Iran's southern coast can engage targets throughout the Strait and into the Gulf of Oman.
unit: hundreds of fast attack craft; dozens of anti-ship missile coastal batteries
political_cost: IRGC Navy operations in the Strait risk provoking Gulf state retaliation and harming Iran's relationships with energy customers; the IRGC's autonomous command structure means operations may proceed without full political authorization from civilian leadership.
source: ONI Iranian Naval Forces guide; IISS Military Balance; CSIS; CENTCOM

name: Naval — Submarine Fleet (Artesh and IRGC)
description: Iran operates three Kilo-class (Project 877EKM) diesel-electric submarines acquired from Russia in the 1990s, which are the most capable conventional submarines in the Persian Gulf region. The Kilo-class can carry torpedoes and potentially anti-ship missiles, and their quiet diesel-electric propulsion makes them difficult to detect in the shallow, acoustically complex waters of the Persian Gulf. Iran also operates numerous midget submarines (Ghadir-class, approximately 120 tons, based on North Korean Yono-class design) and the domestically built Fateh-class (527 tons). The midget submarines are designed for mine-laying, special operations, and torpedo attacks in the confined waters of the Strait. The Artesh Navy controls the Kilo-class submarines while the IRGC Navy operates the midget submarine fleet, creating a dual command structure for subsurface operations. In a conflict, submarines would operate as area-denial assets in the Strait and Gulf of Oman, forcing US carrier groups to maintain significant anti-submarine warfare (ASW) screens that divert resources from strike operations.
unit: Kilo-class SSK (plus ~20 Ghadir midget submarines and several Fateh-class)
political_cost: Submarine operations are relatively low political cost as they are difficult to attribute in real-time and primarily threaten military rather than civilian targets; loss of a Kilo-class submarine would however be irreplaceable given sanctions.
source: ONI; IISS Military Balance; Jane's Fighting Ships

name: IRGC vs. Artesh Command Structure and Mosaic Defense Doctrine
description: Iran's military is divided into two parallel forces: the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC, ~190,000 personnel including ground, naval, aerospace, and Quds Force) and the regular military (Artesh, ~420,000 including army, navy, and air force). The IRGC controls Iran's strategic weapons (ballistic missiles, drones), the proxy network (via Quds Force), Persian Gulf naval operations, and internal security. The Artesh operates conventional forces including the aging air force, blue-water navy, and ground forces. The Supreme Leader commands both through separate chains. Critically, under restructuring completed by 2019, the IRGC adopted a 'Mosaic Defense' doctrine, reorganizing into 31 semi-autonomous provincial commands, each with independent intelligence, weapons stockpiles, and pre-delegated authority to launch strikes if communication with central command is severed. This decentralized architecture is specifically designed to survive decapitation strikes. By January 2026, this doctrine is fully operational—a pre-war US intelligence assessment concluded that large-scale war on Iran would be unlikely to produce regime change because the system prioritizes institutional preservation over any individual leader.
unit: 31 semi-autonomous provincial commands; ~610,000 total military personnel (IRGC + Artesh combined)
political_cost: The dual command structure creates friction—Artesh officers resent IRGC political dominance and resource priority; in wartime, pre-delegated authority to provincial commands risks unauthorized escalation that central leadership cannot control.
source: IISS Military Balance; CTP-ISW assessments; Washington Post (leaked US intelligence assessment); Political and Domestic Dynamics assessment

name: Conventional Air Force (Artesh Air Force)
description: Iran's air force is the weakest branch of its conventional military, operating an aging fleet of pre-1979 Western aircraft and limited post-revolution acquisitions. The inventory includes F-4 Phantom IIs, F-5 Tiger IIs, F-14 Tomcats (unique globally), MiG-29s, and Su-24 Fencer strike aircraft. Most airframes are decades old with limited availability due to sanctions-constrained maintenance and spare parts. Iran compensated for this weakness through its strategic investment in missiles and drones rather than conventional airpower. Russia contracted to deliver 48 Su-35 fighter jets (delivery 2026–2028 timeline) and Yak-130 training jets (at least one squadron delivered by January 2026), along with up to 6 Mi-28 attack helicopters. These Russian acquisitions would represent a qualitative leap but are not yet fully operational. In a conflict with the US and Israel, Iran's air force would be effectively irrelevant—Israel achieved 'absolute aerial freedom of operation in Iranian skies' during the June 2025 war and Iranian fighters detected no SAM launches against incoming Israeli aircraft in Operation Midnight Hammer.
unit: approximately 150–200 combat aircraft of varying readiness; most pre-1979 vintage
political_cost: The air force's irrelevance in modern conflict is a source of institutional humiliation for the Artesh, reinforcing the IRGC's dominance in defense spending and strategic planning.
source: IISS Military Balance; Russian arms transfer reporting; CTP-ISW; FPRI

name: Nuclear Program — Enrichment Status and Breakout Timeline
description: As of January 2026, Iran's nuclear program exists in an unprecedented state of opacity. The June 2025 US/Israeli strikes (Operation Midnight Hammer) severely damaged Fordow, Natanz, and Isfahan—the three declared enrichment sites—using B-2 delivered GBU-57 Massive Ordnance Penetrators and Tomahawk cruise missiles. However, the strikes did not destroy Iran's accumulated fissile material: approximately 440 kg of 60% enriched uranium survived (enough for ~10 weapons if further enriched to 90%). The IAEA assessed that 'almost all sensitive equipment' at Fordow was destroyed, but Iran possesses the 'industrial and technological capabilities' to rebuild in 'a matter of months.' Iran expelled IAEA inspectors in July 2025 and formally suspended all cooperation. As of January 2026, the IAEA 'cannot provide any information on the current size, composition or whereabouts' of Iran's enriched uranium stockpile. Iran is actively constructing 'Pickaxe Mountain,' an underground site near Natanz buried up to 100 meters beneath a mountain, potentially impervious to aerial bombardment. Enrichment from 60% to weapons-grade 90% would take 'a few weeks' (IAEA Director Grossi, September 2025) with even a small number of IR-6 centrifuges. Weaponization would require additional months to a year.
unit: kg of 60% enriched uranium (survived June 2025 strikes; location uncertain)
political_cost: The Supreme Leader's fatwa against nuclear weapons was always disputed in its scope and binding nature; former defense minister Ali Shamkhani publicly stated in October 2025 that he would pursue an atomic bomb if returned to office—indicating the political taboo is eroding within the establishment.
source: IAEA reports (September 2025, February 2026); Arms Control Association; Center for Arms Control; DIA leaked assessment; White House leaked November 2025 document

name: Underground Facilities — Fordow, Natanz, Pickaxe Mountain
description: Iran has invested heavily in underground and hardened facilities to protect its most sensitive nuclear and military assets. Fordow (built inside a mountain near Qom, depth ~80 meters) was designed to withstand aerial bombardment but sustained severe damage from GBU-57 MOPs in June 2025. Natanz (partially underground centrifuge halls) was struck by Tomahawk cruise missiles with confirmed penetration of underground enrichment halls. Isfahan's tunnel complex had entrances collapsed but the deepest underground storage site survived because it exceeded MOP penetration capability. By January 2026, Iran is aggressively constructing Pickaxe Mountain—a new underground complex near Natanz, buried up to 100 meters beneath a mountain taller than Fordow's, specifically designed to be impervious to any known conventional munition including the GBU-57. Satellite imagery shows accelerated construction with equipment potentially transferred from damaged sites. A separate covert site at Minzadehei (northeast of Tehran) has also been identified. These facilities would house reconstituted centrifuge cascades and potentially relocated enriched uranium stocks, creating a breakout pathway that cannot be addressed by air power alone.
unit: 3+ underground/hardened nuclear sites (2 damaged, 1+ under construction, 1+ covert)
political_cost: Underground facility construction is visible to satellite surveillance and signals intent to reconstitute, undermining Iran's diplomatic position; however, the regime calculates that the June 2025 strikes proved vulnerability of surface and shallow-buried sites, making deeper hardening an existential necessity.
source: IAEA reports; satellite imagery analysis (commercial providers); Arms Control Association; Center for Arms Control

name: Russian Military Transfers — Su-35, S-400 Components, Intelligence Sharing
description: Russia has emerged as Iran's primary military patron in the interwar period. Confirmed contracts include 48 Su-35 fighter jets (delivery timeline 2026–2028), Yak-130 training jets (at least one squadron delivered), up to 6 Mi-28 attack helicopters (some delivered by January 2026), and a €500 million deal for 500 Verba MANPADS signed in December 2025. Russia has also delivered S-400 components, a Rezonans-NE over-the-horizon radar, and the Khayyam spy satellite. Most critically, Russia has been providing improved Shahed drone modifications based on combat experience in Ukraine—better targeting, handling, and flight profiles. US intelligence confirms Russia provides Iran with satellite imagery of US warship and aircraft positions. However, both countries consume the same weapons categories (air defense, missiles, drones), and Russia's defense industrial capacity is strained by the Ukraine war, limiting the scale of transfers. The relationship is transactional: Iran supplies drones and missile components for the Ukraine war; Russia supplies advanced conventional weapons and intelligence.
unit: 48 Su-35 on contract; 500 Verba MANPADS; S-400 components; satellite intelligence sharing
political_cost: Russian arms transfers deepen Iran's strategic dependency and complicate any diplomatic settlement with the West; the Verba MANPADS deal was signed just weeks before the February 2026 war, suggesting both sides anticipated conflict.
source: US intelligence sources (multiple outlets); CTP-ISW; IISS; Defense Security Asia

name: Proxy — Hezbollah (Post-2024 Lebanon War)
description: Hezbollah suffered devastating losses during the 2024 Lebanon conflict, including the assassination of Secretary General Hassan Nasrallah and IRGC Brigadier General Abbas Nilforoushan in September 2024, along with much of its senior military leadership. Israel's multi-month campaign in Lebanon degraded Hezbollah's rocket stockpile (pre-war estimated at 130,000–150,000), destroyed significant portions of its tunnel infrastructure in southern Lebanon, and disrupted its command structure. A ceasefire was eventually reached in late 2024. By January 2026, Hezbollah retains significant residual capability—it remains the most heavily armed non-state actor in the world—but is substantially weakened compared to its pre-October 2023 peak. Reconstruction and rearming are underway with Iranian support, but Israeli surveillance and periodic strikes constrain the pace. In a renewed conflict, Hezbollah could still launch approximately 100 rockets per day against Israel, opening a critical northern front that forces Israel to divide its air defense and ground forces. Hezbollah's institutional resilience and deep social base in Lebanon's Shia community ensure its survival as an organization even after severe military degradation.
unit: residual rocket arsenal in the tens of thousands (degraded from 130,000–150,000 pre-2024); ~20,000–30,000 fighters estimated
political_cost: Hezbollah re-entering a conflict on Iran's behalf would devastate Lebanon again—886+ killed and 1 million displaced during the March 2026 Israeli invasion demonstrates the cost; Lebanese public opposition to another war constrains but does not prevent Hezbollah's participation.
source: IISS; CSIS; Carnegie Middle East Center; Israeli military assessments; UNSC reports

name: Proxy — Houthi Movement (Ansar Allah)
description: The Houthis demonstrated remarkable offensive capability during 2024, conducting unprecedented anti-shipping attacks in the Red Sea and launching long-range drone and missile strikes against Israel. However, they suffered severe degradation through 2025: Israel killed 12 Houthi cabinet members in August 2025, and sustained US/coalition strikes degraded their military infrastructure. The Houthis paused Red Sea attacks following the Gaza ceasefire in October 2025, and a reported May 2025 deal with Washington plus Saudi diplomatic understandings appear to be constraining their posture. By January 2026, the Houthis represent a latent rather than active threat—they retain significant capability including long-range drones and anti-ship missiles, but leadership has been decimated and the organization appears to be exercising strategic patience. In a major Iran-US/Israel conflict, the Houthis could re-enter by resuming Red Sea attacks, targeting Saudi Arabia and UAE, and launching drones toward Israel. Their geographic distance from the primary theater makes them difficult to fully neutralize, and any resumption would compound the Strait of Hormuz crisis with a Red Sea crisis.
unit: anti-ship missile batteries; drone stockpiles; ~30,000+ fighters; exact arsenal unknown post-2025 strikes
political_cost: Houthi re-engagement risks drawing Saudi Arabia into the conflict and collapsing the May 2025 de-escalation framework; however, if Iran's survival is at stake, these considerations become secondary.
source: UK maritime authorities; CENTCOM; CTP-ISW; Israeli military assessments of Houthi leadership degradation

name: Proxy — Islamic Resistance in Iraq (IRI) / Iranian-Backed Iraqi Militias
description: The Islamic Resistance in Iraq (IRI) is a coalition of Iran-backed Shia militia groups operating under the umbrella of Iraq's Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF), with approximately 100,000 PMF personnel total. Key factions include Kataib Hezbollah, Asa'ib Ahl al-Haq, Kata'ib Sayyid al-Shuhada, and Harakat Hezbollah al-Nujaba. These groups possess Iranian-supplied short-range ballistic missiles, drones (including Shahed variants), rockets, and anti-tank guided missiles. They have extensive experience from low-level attacks on US bases in Iraq and Syria throughout 2023–2024. Their geographic proximity to US military installations (Al-Asad, Victory Base, Erbil, Baghdad's Green Zone) makes them an immediate threat. In a major conflict, Iraqi militias provide Iran with a second front that forces the US to divert forces to protect bases and the embassy in Baghdad. The IRI's stated objective is to 'drag the US into a long war of attrition.' Their capability was validated by the sustained campaign of over 300 attacks following the February 2026 strikes, but even in January 2026 pre-war conditions, these groups maintained high readiness and demonstrated willingness to escalate.
unit: PMF-affiliated personnel (not all directly Iran-controlled; core IRI fighters estimated at 10,000–30,000)
political_cost: Iraqi militia attacks on US forces strain Iraq's sovereignty and risk provoking a broader US military response inside Iraq; Baghdad's government is caught between its security dependence on PMF and its desire to maintain relations with Washington.
source: CTP-ISW; CENTCOM; IISS; CRS reports on Iran-backed militias in Iraq

name: Non-Aligned Movement and Global South Framing
description: Iran has cultivated relationships across the Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) and the broader Global South, positioning itself as a victim of Western/Israeli aggression and champion of anti-imperialism. The October 2024 strikes and June 2025 war reinforced this narrative among many developing nations. Algeria condemned the October 2024 strikes; numerous African, Asian, and Latin American states issued statements of solidarity. Iran leverages its membership in various multilateral forums to rally diplomatic support—while this does not translate into material military assistance, it creates political costs for the US in international institutions (UNGA votes, ICC referrals, UNSC debates). China's public condemnation of the June 2025 strikes as lacking UN authorization and violating international law amplifies this framing. In a renewed conflict, Iran would aggressively frame US/Israeli military action as illegal aggression against a sovereign state, particularly if strikes hit civilian infrastructure or nuclear facilities. The February 27, 2026 diplomatic breakthrough—Iran's agreement to nuclear verification—was specifically designed to create this narrative trap: any subsequent military action would appear to undermine diplomacy.
unit: 120+ NAM member states; diplomatic alignment, not military alliance
political_cost: Iran's human rights record (particularly the January 2026 domestic protest massacres), proxy activities, and nuclear opacity undermine its diplomatic credibility; Global South support is broad but shallow—no country will provide material military assistance.
source: NAM membership records; UNGA voting patterns; Chinese MFA statements; Carnegie Endowment analysis

name: SCO Membership and China/Russia Strategic Partnerships
description: Iran became a full member of the Shanghai Cooperation Organisation (SCO) in 2023, providing it with a formal multilateral framework linking it to China and Russia. This membership offers diplomatic legitimacy, potential economic integration pathways, and a counterweight to Western isolation. The Russia relationship is now deeply militarized, with confirmed arms transfers, intelligence sharing, and technology cooperation as detailed in the military capabilities section. The China relationship is primarily economic—China imports 80–90% of Iran's oil exports (1.38 million bpd in 2025 at an $8–10/barrel discount) and provides critical dual-use technology including access to the BeiDou-3 encrypted satellite navigation system. China also pre-war exported radar systems, navigation technology, and electronic warfare tools. However, both relationships have limits: Russia's defense industrial capacity is strained by Ukraine, and China prioritizes its economic relationship with the US over its partnership with Iran. Neither is likely to intervene militarily on Iran's behalf.
unit: full SCO membership; bilateral strategic partnerships with Russia and China
political_cost: Deep dependence on China and Russia undermines Iran's claims of non-aligned independence; China's willingness to leverage its relationship during crisis (pressing Iran on Hormuz to protect Chinese energy flows) reveals the power imbalance.
source: SCO membership records; Chinese trade data; BeiDou-3 access reporting; SCMP; Reuters; CTP-ISW

name: February 27 Diplomatic Breakthrough — Nuclear Verification Agreement
description: On February 27, 2026, Oman's Foreign Minister announced a diplomatic breakthrough: Iran agreed to transfer enriched uranium abroad and accept full IAEA verification. This came after months of backchannel negotiations and represented Iran's most significant diplomatic concession since the original JCPOA. The timing was critical—the announcement was made while the US had already completed its largest military buildup in the Middle East since the 2003 Iraq invasion. Iranian leadership may have calculated that the agreement would forestall military action, while US/Israeli planners had already obtained Khamenei's location (Netanyahu called Trump on February 23 to share it). The agreement's existence creates massive diplomatic and legitimacy costs for any subsequent military action: attacking a country one day after it agreed to nuclear verification undermines the stated casus belli and damages US credibility in future nonproliferation negotiations. For Iran's diplomatic strategy in any conflict, this sequence of events—agreement followed immediately by decapitation strike—is the single most powerful narrative weapon available, framing the conflict as unprovoked aggression against a country seeking peace.
unit: diplomatic agreement (announced but not implemented before Feb 28 strikes)
political_cost: Iran's diplomatic credibility is itself constrained by its July 2025 expulsion of IAEA inspectors, January 2026 domestic massacre of protesters, and years of nuclear obfuscation; the breakthrough may have been a negotiating tactic rather than genuine compliance.
source: Oman FM announcement; US diplomatic reporting; IAEA Director Grossi statements; GeoSim PRD scenario documentation

name: Oil Exports and Chinese Energy Dependency
description: Iran exported approximately 1.38 million barrels per day of crude oil in 2025, with 80–90% going to China at an $8–10/barrel discount to market prices. Pre-war stockpiling was aggressive: Iran exported 2.16 mbd in February 2026, the highest level since July 2018. Iran's oil sector is the regime's economic lifeline, accounting for the vast majority of foreign exchange earnings despite comprehensive US sanctions. Sanctions evasion relies on a 'shadow fleet' of tankers with obscured ownership, AIS transponder manipulation, ship-to-ship transfers, and Chinese financial intermediaries that process payments in yuan rather than dollars. Kharg Island handles approximately 90% of Iranian crude exports. Iran's economic vulnerability is severe: 45% inflation, 25% youth unemployment, and foreign currency reserves depleted by years of sanctions and military expenditure. In a conflict, Iran's oil infrastructure—particularly Kharg Island—becomes both a target and a leverage point. Iran can threaten Hormuz closure to hold hostage 20 million bpd of global oil flows, but this simultaneously cuts off its own export revenue, creating a mutual vulnerability.
unit: barrels per day of crude oil exports (2025 average; surged to 2.16 mbd in February 2026)
political_cost: Oil revenue sustains both regime social spending and IRGC military programs; any disruption to exports intensifies the already severe economic crisis driving domestic protests and regime instability.
source: Chinese customs data; SCMP; Reuters; Bloomberg; TankerTrackers.com; economic assessment documents

name: Strait of Hormuz Closure Threat — Iran's Strategic Economic Weapon
description: The Strait of Hormuz is the world's most critical energy chokepoint, with approximately 20 million barrels per day of crude oil and significant LNG volumes transiting daily—roughly 20% of global oil supply and 25% of global LNG. Iran controls the northern shore and islands within the Strait. Closure or restriction of the Strait is Iran's single most powerful strategic lever: bypass pipeline alternatives (Saudi East-West Pipeline, UAE's ADCOP pipeline, Iran's Goreh-Jask pipeline) can handle only 3.5–5.5 million bpd combined, leaving 15–17 million bpd unaddressed. There is no bypass for refined products or LNG. Iran's Hormuz denial toolkit includes mine warfare (5,000–6,000 mines), anti-ship coastal missile batteries, fast attack craft swarms, submarines, and drone attacks on commercial shipping. However, Iran is itself vulnerable to Hormuz closure: its own oil exports transit the Strait, and its southern coast population centers depend on imports. Approximately 84% of oil and 83% of LNG shipped through Hormuz is bound for Asia, making China, Japan, South Korea, and India the primary economic victims. This creates a complex deterrence dynamic where Iran's threat to close Hormuz simultaneously harms its most important economic partner (China).
unit: barrels per day of oil transiting the Strait (plus significant LNG volumes)
political_cost: Hormuz closure would devastate the global economy, including nations sympathetic to Iran; China has explicitly pressured Iran on this point, and actual closure risks alienating Iran's most important remaining partner.
source: EIA Strait of Hormuz analysis; IISS; CSIS; IEA; economic assessment documents on bypass capacity

name: Sanctions Evasion Infrastructure
description: Iran has developed sophisticated sanctions evasion mechanisms over decades of US primary and secondary sanctions. Key elements include: a shadow fleet of tankers (estimated 400+ vessels) with obscured ownership structures, frequent flag changes, and AIS transponder manipulation to disguise cargo origins and destinations; Chinese financial intermediaries that process oil payments in yuan through banks not exposed to US correspondent banking; front companies across UAE, Turkey, Iraq, and Southeast Asia for procurement of dual-use goods; cryptocurrency transactions for smaller-value transfers; and barter arrangements where oil is exchanged for Chinese manufactured goods. The system is remarkably resilient—Iran continued exporting 1.38 mbd even under 'maximum pressure' sanctions. However, the shadow fleet and financial networks are vulnerable to intelligence disruption, secondary sanctions enforcement against Chinese banks (which the US has been reluctant to pursue due to broader economic implications), and wartime interdiction of tanker routes. Iran's foreign currency reserves are estimated at $20–30 billion, severely depleted from pre-JCPOA levels of $100+ billion.
unit: ~$20–30 billion estimated foreign reserves; 400+ shadow fleet vessels
political_cost: Sanctions evasion requires cooperation from Chinese state-adjacent banks and enterprises; any US decision to enforce secondary sanctions on these entities would create a US-China confrontation that Iran cannot control.
source: US Treasury sanctions designations; TankerTrackers.com; Reuters and Bloomberg reporting on shadow fleet; CRS sanctions reports

name: Domestic Economic Crisis — Inflation, Unemployment, Protests
description: Iran's economy is in severe distress as of January 2026. Key indicators include approximately 45% inflation, 25% youth unemployment, a currency that has lost roughly 80% of its value against the dollar since 2018, and widening income inequality. GDP growth is anemic despite oil exports, as sanctions prevent access to international capital markets and technology. The economic crisis was a primary driver of the massive protests in January 2026, during which the regime killed thousands of its own citizens (estimated 5,000–36,500 depending on source) to maintain control. Pre-war polling (GAMAAN 2024) showed approximately 70% of Iranians opposed continuation of the Islamic Republic, 89% supported democracy, and 65% planned to boycott elections. This domestic fragility is a critical constraint on Iran's conflict calculus: prolonged war would worsen economic conditions and risk renewed unrest, but the regime calculates that wartime conditions justify even more repressive measures. Carnegie Endowment assessed that any rally-around-the-flag effect from the June 2025 war 'rapidly dissipated' before the current crisis.
unit: 45% inflation; 25% youth unemployment; ~70% opposition to regime (GAMAAN 2024 polling)
political_cost: The regime's primary domestic mechanism is repression, not consent; sustaining both a major external conflict and internal control simultaneously strains the IRGC's capacity and risks security force defections if the situation becomes sufficiently desperate.
source: GAMAAN polling (2024); Carnegie Endowment; IMF data; World Bank; reporting on January 2026 protest suppression

name: MOIS (Ministry of Intelligence and Security)
description: MOIS is Iran's primary civilian intelligence agency, responsible for foreign intelligence collection, domestic counterintelligence, and monitoring of dissident groups both inside Iran and in the diaspora. MOIS operates a global network of agents and informants, with particular strength in Iraq, Lebanon, Afghanistan, and European diaspora communities. It is institutionally distinct from and sometimes competitive with IRGC Intelligence. MOIS has conducted extraterritorial assassination plots against dissidents and opposition figures (multiple plots disrupted in Europe and the US in recent years), maintains liaison relationships with allied intelligence services (including Hezbollah's intelligence apparatus), and conducts strategic intelligence collection against Gulf state, Israeli, and US targets. In a conflict, MOIS would coordinate intelligence support to proxy forces, conduct sabotage operations against adversary interests in third countries, and manage deception and denial operations regarding Iran's nuclear program. Its capabilities are professional but have been penetrated repeatedly by Israeli and US intelligence, as demonstrated by the precision of the June 2025 and February 2026 strikes.
unit: agency-level capability; global human intelligence network
political_cost: MOIS assassination plots in Europe have resulted in diplomatic expulsions and strengthened the Western case against Iran; extraterritorial operations risk alienating neutral states Iran needs as diplomatic partners.
source: IISS; US DOJ indictments of MOIS operatives; European law enforcement reports; CRS

name: IRGC Intelligence Organization (IRGC-IO)
description: The IRGC Intelligence Organization is the parallel intelligence service controlled by the Revolutionary Guards, focused on regime security, proxy network coordination, and offensive intelligence operations. It is more ideologically driven than MOIS and has direct operational connections to Quds Force external operations. IRGC-IO controls intelligence sharing with proxy groups, manages covert procurement networks for weapons and dual-use technology, and conducts offensive cyber operations through affiliated units. The organization played a central role in coordinating Iran's April and October 2024 strikes on Israel and the June 2025 war response. In the interwar period, IRGC-IO has been involved in relocating nuclear materials and equipment to concealed sites, managing the reconstitution of military capabilities, and maintaining communications with the 31 autonomous provincial commands under the Mosaic Defense doctrine. The tension between IRGC-IO and MOIS reflects the broader IRGC vs. civilian government power struggle, with IRGC-IO gaining increasing dominance as the security situation escalates.
unit: agency-level capability embedded within IRGC command structure
political_cost: IRGC-IO's dominance over MOIS reflects the militarization of Iranian governance; intelligence operations that cross into provocative territory (e.g., assassination plots, cyberattacks on civilian infrastructure) can escalate conflicts beyond regime intentions.
source: CTP-ISW; IISS; US intelligence community assessments; Quds Force reporting

name: Cyber Warfare Capabilities (APT33, APT35, CyberAv3ngers, MuddyWater)
description: Iran possesses significant offensive cyber capabilities operated by IRGC-affiliated groups with established track records documented in US government attribution and private sector threat intelligence. Key groups include APT33/Elfin (targeting aviation and energy sectors, attributed to IRGC), APT35/Charming Kitten (spear-phishing campaigns against think tanks, journalists, and government officials), CyberAv3ngers (IRGC-backed, attacked US water treatment facilities and industrial control systems in 2023–2024), and MuddyWater (intelligence collection against Middle Eastern and European targets). Historical operations include the 2012 Shamoon attack on Saudi Aramco (wiping 30,000+ workstations), the 2012–2013 DDoS campaign against US financial institutions, and the 2024 attacks on US water treatment plant PLCs. Symantec found evidence of Iranian backdoors pre-installed on US company networks. Capabilities include destructive malware, ransomware, supply chain compromise, and industrial control system (ICS) attacks. In a major conflict, Iran would activate pre-positioned access across US, Israeli, and Gulf state critical infrastructure—targeting power grids, water systems, financial networks, and oil/gas SCADA systems. These capabilities are force-multiplied by coordination with pro-Russian hacktivist groups.
unit: multiple APT groups; pre-positioned network access across critical infrastructure sectors
political_cost: Cyberattacks on civilian infrastructure (hospitals, water treatment, power grids) risk crossing red lines that justify kinetic retaliation and undermine Iran's diplomatic narrative; attribution is increasingly rapid, reducing deniability.
source: US CISA advisories; FBI/NSA joint advisories; Symantec/Mandiant/CrowdStrike threat intelligence; DOJ indictments; Saudi Aramco incident reporting

name: Chinese BeiDou-3 and Russian Satellite Intelligence Access
description: Iran has access to China's BeiDou-3 encrypted military satellite navigation system, granted in 2021, providing precision guidance capability independent of GPS (which the US can selectively deny or spoof). Experts assess Iran is using BeiDou-3 to guide drone and missile attacks. Separately, Russia provides Iran with satellite imagery from its military reconnaissance constellation, including real-time positions of US warships and aircraft. Russia also delivered the Khayyam spy satellite to Iran in 2022, providing indigenous imaging capability. These intelligence feeds are critical force multipliers: they enable Iran to target mobile assets (carrier groups, deployed air defense batteries) that would otherwise be beyond its organic sensor network's reach. The combination of BeiDou precision navigation for weapons guidance and Russian satellite imagery for targeting creates a distributed kill chain that does not depend on Iran's own degraded radar and reconnaissance infrastructure. In a conflict, degradation of these external intelligence feeds—through diplomatic pressure on Russia/China or technical countermeasures—would significantly reduce the effectiveness of Iran's precision-strike capabilities.
unit: access to BeiDou-3 military signal; Russian satellite imagery feed; Khayyam spy satellite
political_cost: Dependence on Chinese and Russian intelligence creates strategic vulnerability: either patron could restrict access as leverage, and China in particular might limit cooperation if US-China tensions demand de-escalation.
source: US intelligence community reporting; BeiDou-3 access confirmed in 2021; Russian satellite imagery sharing confirmed by multiple US officials; Khayyam satellite launch reporting
